title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

texts.A: [40]Cultivated meat represents a genuine and profound technological miracle that possesses the real potential to finally end the immense and largely invisible suffering endured by billions of animals in the global factory farming industry.[/40] [39]By carefully growing real, authentic animal cells in a precisely controlled bioreactor environment, we can provide the rapidly growing world population with the protein and nutrition it requires without perpetuating the environmental catastrophe and deep ethical nightmare of industrial-scale slaughterhouses.[/39] Critics who reflexively dismiss this technology as 'unnatural' are conveniently ignoring the inconvenient fact that our current industrial meat production system, with its antibiotics, artificial growth hormones, and cramped feedlots, is anything but natural in any meaningful sense. It is a deeply unnatural system masquerading as tradition. [37]Cultivated meat is the only genuinely viable and scalable path toward a more sustainable future—one in which we can remain carnivores by preference without being moral killers by necessity.[/37]
texts.B: The considerable and well-funded hype surrounding lab-grown meat represents, in my considered view, a genuinely dangerous and strategic distraction from the urgent and morally necessary transition toward a predominantly plant-based global diet as our primary response to the food system crisis. The real and enduring solution is not a sophisticated high-technology replication of the original problem. [37]Cellular agriculture as currently practised is an extraordinarily energy-intensive industrial process which, if implemented at the truly global scale required to make any meaningful difference to agricultural emissions, could plausibly carry a larger net carbon footprint than thoughtfully managed and certified traditional regenerative grazing systems that sequester carbon in healthy soils.[/37] Furthermore, [38]I share the deep and legitimate concern that this exclusively proprietary technology would enable a small and already powerful number of biotech corporations to patent and effectively monopolize our global food supply, moving us even further from genuine food sovereignty and meaningful local self-determination than we already uncomfortably are.[/38] We fundamentally need to reconnect with the soil through genuinely regenerative local agriculture.
texts.C: I agree that the potential for dangerous corporate monopolization of the cultivated meat industry represents a significant and legitimate long-term risk that demands proactive regulatory attention and robust policy frameworks. However, we must also be pragmatic and honest about the scale of the challenge. Billions of people around the world are not going to voluntarily and rapidly abandon meat consumption overnight, regardless of the moral or environmental arguments made. [39]I share the compelling view that 'cultured' meat technology represents an absolutely vital and practical tool for substantially and measurably reducing the immense scale of unnecessary animal cruelty in our current food system.[/39] However, the current cost of production remains truly astronomical, and the long-term biological safety profile of the chemical growth mediums routinely employed in current bioreactor systems remains a genuinely unresolved area of scientific uncertainty. [38]We should actively and generously support this promising research while ensuring it remains firmly in the public domain rather than locked behind proprietary corporate patents.[/38]
texts.D: Lab-grown meat is ultimately a sophisticated and expensive technological 'fix' designed for a problem that we fundamentally should not have created in the first place. It addresses the visible symptoms of our profoundly broken global food system while carefully avoiding any serious engagement with its deeper structural causes: overconsumption, commodification, and the complete disconnection of the urban consumer from the ecological realities of food production. I share the widespread skepticism regarding the actual environmental benefits; the truly massive and continuous energy requirements of industrial bioreactors operating at commercial scale are consistently and conveniently overlooked in the slick marketing materials of startup investors. While I genuinely acknowledge that cultured meat would substantially reduce direct animal slaughter, I believe our collective focus should be directed toward the 're-wilding' of agricultural land and a genuine, structural reduction in total meat consumption across wealthy societies. [40]True and lasting sustainability is not discovered in a sterile laboratory; it is painstakingly cultivated through a genuinely balanced and reciprocal relationship with the living world.[/40]

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on whether lab-grown meat is the best environmental solution?
questions.explanation: Expert A calls it 'the only path toward a sustainable future.' Expert B argues it is 'energy-intensive' and could have a larger carbon footprint than regenerative grazing.

questions.question: Which expert shares B's concern about the ownership and control of food technology?
questions.explanation: Expert B mentions 'biotech giants patenting our food supply.' Expert C agrees, noting the risk of 'corporate control' and 'corporate patents.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to A regarding the ethical impact on animal welfare?
questions.explanation: Expert A focuses on ending 'immense suffering' and 'slaughterhouses.' Expert C also shares the view that it is a 'vital tool for reducing animal cruelty.'

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from D on the role of laboratories in the future of food?
questions.explanation: Expert D argues that 'true sustainability isn't found in a laboratory.' Expert A, conversely, sees the laboratory (and cultivated meat) as a 'technological miracle' and the only path forward.
